original_hypothesis.title: RIP-7212 Precompile False Positive on Chains with Arbitrary Code at Address 0x100
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (VERIFIER) for RIP-7212 precompile signature verification. If no code exists at 0x100, staticcall returns success=true with empty returndata, and the code falls through to FCL (safe). However, on target chains where a regular contract exists at address 0x100 (any of: Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis), the staticcall may return arbitrary data. If ret.length > 0 and abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1, the function returns true immediately, accepting any forged passkey signature without falling through to FCL verification.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code checks `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1` but does not verify that the address contains the actual RIP-7212 precompile vs arbitrary code. On any target chain where 0x100 has deployed bytecode that happens to return 32 bytes decoding to 1 (or can be made to), all passkey-owner signatures would be trivially forgeable, enabling complete wallet takeover.
deployment_assumptions: RIP-7212 precompile is correctly deployed at 0x100 on all target chains, OR no contract code exists at 0x100
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Target chains include Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis per documentation. No deployment evidence verifying 0x100 status on each chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall to hardcoded VERIFIER=0x100 with only ret.length>0 check; no validation that 0x100 is actually RIP-7212 precompile vs arbitrary contract

preconditions: Target chain has deployed contract code at address 0x100 that is NOT the RIP-7212 precompile
Said contract returns 32+ bytes of data that decodes to uint256(1) when called with the abi.encode(messageHash, r, s, x, y) payload
Wallet has at least one passkey (secp256r1) owner registered

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Trusting staticcall return data from unverified address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bool valid = ret.length > 0; if (success && valid) return abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1; — falls through to FCL only if precompile returns empty data or call fails
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No chain-specific check for RIP-7212 deployment, no code-size check on VERIFIER address

analysis_questions: On which target chains does address 0x100 have deployed code?
What does the contract at 0x100 return when called with the 5-word ABI-encoded payload?
Could an attacker deploy code at 0x100 on any target chain via CREATE2?
validation_requirements: Check code size at 0x100 on all 8 target chains
Test staticcall behavior with forged signature data on chains with code at 0x100
Verify FCL fallback is never reached when precompile returns exploitable data

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a target chain where 0x100 returns favorable data. They craft a WebAuthnAuth struct with arbitrary r,s values, submit a UserOp signed with this forged passkey signature, validateUserOp accepts it via the WebAuthn path, and the attacker gains full wallet control to drain all funds.
prerequisites: Contract code at 0x100 on target chain
Passkey owner registered on victim wallet

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete wallet takeover — all ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs drained
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallets using passkey owners on affected chains
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — attacker can also add their own owner and remove existing ones

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No cross-chain deployment verification for VERIFIER address

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect proactively without chain-specific audits

validation_result.impact_summary: On any chain where address 0x100 hosts non-RIP-7212 code that returns 32 bytes decoding to uint256(1), all passkey signature verifications are bypassed, enabling complete wallet takeover.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The WebAuthn.verify function performs a staticcall to hardcoded address 0x100 (VERIFIER) and trusts its return data without any validation that the address actually contains the RIP-7212 precompile. The code only checks `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1`. If a non-precompile contract exists at 0x100 on any target chain and returns 32 bytes decoding to 1 for any input, the function immediately returns true without falling through to the FCL software verification. This is a missing safeguard—there is no EXTCODESIZE check, no chain-specific configuration, and no validation that the responder is the intended precompile. The code comments acknowledge the empty-address case ('staticcall will not revert if address has no code') but do not address the arbitrary-code-at-0x100 case, confirming the gap in defensive checks.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: WebAuthn.sol lines 159-165 clearly show the vulnerable pattern. VERIFIER is defined at line 48 as `address private constant VERIFIER = address(0x100)`. The staticcall at line 159 sends args to this address. Line 163 checks `ret.length > 0` and line 164 returns `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1` if both success and valid are true. No EXTCODESIZE check, no chain validation, and no verification that the response came from a legitimate RIP-7212 implementation. The FCL fallback at line 166 is only reached if the staticcall returns empty data or fails.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The staticcall to VERIFIER (0x100) trusts the return data without verifying the address contains the RIP-7212 precompile. If arbitrary code at 0x100 returns 32 bytes decoding to uint256(1), the function returns true immediately, bypassing FCL signature verification entirely. No EXTCODESIZE or chain-awareness check exists.

calling_sequence: 4. For 64-byte owner (passkey), decodes WebAuthnAuth and calls WebAuthn.verify at line 277-278
5. WebAuthn.verify performs staticcall to 0x100 at line 159
6. If 0x100 has non-precompile code returning 32 bytes == uint256(1), line 164 returns true
7. FCL.ecdsa_verify at line 166 is NEVER reached
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: validateUserOp → _validateSignature → WebAuthn.verify → VERIFIER.staticcall(args). At WebAuthn.sol:163-164, `bool valid = ret.length > 0; if (success && valid) return abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1;` — if the contract at 0x100 returns favorable data, the signature is accepted as valid without any cryptographic verification by FCL. The attacker can forge any WebAuthnAuth struct with arbitrary r,s values since neither the precompile (non-existent) nor FCL (skipped) validates the signature.

deployment_assumptions_checked: RIP-7212 precompile is correctly deployed at 0x100 on all target chains, OR no contract code exists at 0x100
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The foundry.toml configuration contains only basic project settings (src, out, libs, remappings, fs_permissions). No deployment scripts, chain configuration, or 0x100 verification logic is provided. There is no evidence of any chain-specific deployment guards or precompile verification steps.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker identifies a target chain where: (a) CoinbaseSmartWallet is deployed, (b) address 0x100 contains non-RIP-7212 code that returns uint256(1). The attacker crafts a UserOperation targeting a victim wallet that has a passkey owner. The UserOp.signature contains a SignatureWrapper pointing to the passkey owner's index, with arbitrary r,s values in the WebAuthnAuth struct (and valid authenticatorData/clientDataJSON structure passing the type/challenge/UP checks). When validateUserOp processes this, WebAuthn.verify's staticcall to 0x100 returns data decoding to 1, so the signature is accepted. The attacker gains full wallet control to drain funds, add their own owners, or upgrade the implementation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If exploitable on any target chain, this enables complete wallet takeover for ALL wallets using passkey owners on that chain. The attacker can: drain all ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs; add their own owner address; remove all existing owners; and upgrade the implementation to a malicious contract. Recovery is impossible once the attacker controls the wallet. The severity is CRITICAL but conditional on chain-specific state at 0x100.
recommended_mitigations: Add an EXTCODESIZE check: only trust 0x100 return data on chains where the address is known to be a precompile (precompiles have no EVM bytecode, so extcodesize(0x100) == 0 for a legitimate precompile)
Use a configurable or chain-specific verifier address instead of hardcoding 0x100
Always verify with FCL as a secondary check regardless of precompile response, or require both to agree
Add a constructor/initializer parameter or immutable flag to enable/disable the precompile path per deployment chain
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a contract at 0x100 on a local fork that always returns abi.encode(uint256(1)). 2. Deploy CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory and create a wallet with a passkey owner. 3. Craft a UserOperation with arbitrary r,s in WebAuthnAuth but valid authenticatorData/clientDataJSON. 4. Submit the UserOp via EntryPoint and verify it passes validateUserOp. 5. Confirm the forged signature grants execution authority.
uncertainties: Current state of address 0x100 on all target chains (Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis, Base, Optimism, Arbitrum) is unknown
Whether an attacker can deploy code at 0x100 on any of these chains (depends on whether the address is reachable via CREATE/CREATE2 and chain-specific protections)
Some chains may protect low addresses from regular contract deployment
The actual RIP-7212 adoption status across target chains affects exploitability
additional_testing_needed: Check EXTCODESIZE at 0x100 on all target chains
Determine if CREATE2 can target 0x100 on each chain
Test staticcall behavior to 0x100 with forged signature data on each chain
Verify whether EVM precompile-range protections exist on target chains that prevent code deployment at 0x100
